title: VerificationReport/Finding schema and render gating
short_description: Publish the VerificationReport/Finding JSON schema (stable namespaced codes, severity, beat/node ids, t_window, expected/actual, fix_hint, rule_version) as snapshot-tested public API, render the human format from the same report object, and gate anima render on error-severity findings with a --force metadata trace.
long_description_md:
# VerificationReport/Finding schema and render gating

## Why this task exists

anima-ai's entire pitch is "verify passes ⇒ the video is correct, no human has to watch it." That claim lives or dies on one artifact: the `VerificationReport`. Every verification rule the project ever ships — structural checks, layout/readability rules, taste constraints, map validity, subtitle contrast, and whatever rules get added after a human catches something verify missed (product-spec success metric #2) — is only useful to an agent if it lands in one stable, machine-readable, namespaced shape that never quietly changes meaning under it. This task builds that shape, and the two things that consume it in Phase 2: a human-readable formatter and the render-gate policy.

This is deliberately narrow. It does **not** implement any actual verification rule (`REF.*`, `OVERLAP.TEXT_TEXT`, `TIMING.READ_TIME`, `MAP.UNKNOWN_TERRITORY`, …) — those are separate Phase 2 tasks (structural checks; layout/readability rules) that populate `findings[]` by constructing objects against the schema this task defines. Building the schema and the rule set in the same task would couple a stable public contract to fast-moving rule logic and make the contract harder to snapshot-lock early, which is exactly the trap `data-model.md`'s report-stability invariant ("finding codes are append-only; a code's meaning never changes") is designed to avoid.

## Roadmap fit

`docs/cofounder/ROADMAP.md`, Phase 2 ("Verification engine v1 + subtitles"), done-when bullet 3, verbatim: *"VerificationReport JSON schema published and snapshot-tested; human output rendered from the same report object; `render` gates on error-severity findings (`--force` leaves a metadata trace)."* That is this task's exact scope — the other Phase 2 bullets (structural checks, layout/readability rules, subtitles, performance budgets, agent-in-the-loop regression suite) are separate tasks that build on top of what ships here.

Depends on:
- **`scene-graph-model`** — `Finding.node_ids`/`beat_ids` reference the stable typed IDs that task defines, and this task's versioned JSON codec (`schema_version`, canonical serialization, snapshot-fixture discipline, strict-mode round-tripping) follows the exact contract pattern that task established for the scene graph, so the two public JSON contracts in the codebase behave consistently for downstream tooling and diffing.
- **`cli-framework`** — render gating reuses the CLI's typed exit-code contract (0 success / 1 internal error / 2 user error / 3 verification failure), its `--json`/stdout-data-vs-stderr-diagnostics separation, and its global exception boundary. The gate is implemented as a policy module the not-yet-built `anima render` command calls into, not as ad hoc logic bolted onto render.

## Core model (per `data-model.md`)

**`VerificationReport`**: `project` (name), `engine_version`, `pack_version`, `schema_version` (the report contract's own version, independent of `engine_version` — same separation scene-graph-model established for its JSON contract), `counts` (findings grouped by severity), `findings` (ordered list of `Finding`), `pass` (bool — true iff zero error-severity findings; this is a derived/computed field, never independently settable, so a report can never claim `pass=true` while carrying an error finding).

**`Finding`**: `code` (stable, namespaced, dot-separated string such as `OVERLAP.TEXT_TEXT`, `TIMING.READ_TIME`, `MAP.UNKNOWN_TERRITORY`, `CONTINUITY.COLOR_DRIFT` — the namespace prefix groups rule families for filtering and docs, e.g. `OVERLAP.*`, `TIMING.*`, `MAP.*`, `REF.*`, `CONTINUITY.*`, `PACING.*`, `STYLE.*`, `BUILD.*`), `severity` (`error` | `warning` — no other values; promote/demote per-project severity config is explicitly Phase 5 scope per ROADMAP, not this task), `beat_ids` (ordered list of stable beat IDs involved, may be empty), `node_ids` (ordered list of stable node IDs involved, may be empty), `t_window` (optional resolved `[start, end]` time interval on the project timeline the finding pertains to; null when the finding isn't time-scoped, e.g. a structural reference error), `expected` (JSON-representable value or description of the correct state), `actual` (JSON-representable value or description of what was found), `fix_hint` (required, non-empty, concrete and actionable string — never a generic "fix this" placeholder; the product-spec's cited +37–40pp agent-task-completion evidence for structured repair suggestions is the design basis, so `fix_hint` is not optional and is validated non-empty at construction), `rule_version` (the version of the specific rule implementation that produced this finding, independent of both `schema_version` and `engine_version`, so a rule's internal threshold tuning is distinguishable from a report-contract change).

Both types must be immutable value objects (matching the scene-graph-model precedent) so a report handed to the CLI, the render gate, or a snapshot test cannot be mutated after construction.

## Namespaced codes and the append-only policy

Codes are the report's most load-bearing detail: agents pattern-match on `code` to select a fix strategy, and `data-model.md` invariant 6 states codes are append-only — a code's meaning never changes once shipped; a rule that needs a breaking semantic change ships a **new** code and deprecates the old one, it never silently repurposes an existing one. This task must:

- Define the code format as a validated pattern (`UPPER_NAMESPACE.UPPER_RULE`, e.g. `OVERLAP.TEXT_TEXT`), enforced at `Finding` construction so a malformed or unnamespaced code is a typed programming error at build time, not something that reaches a report.
- Ship a small, versioned code registry/enum mechanism that downstream rule tasks (structural checks, layout/readability rules, and everything after) register new codes into, with a test that fails the build if an existing registered code's severity-class or description is silently redefined rather than deprecated. No real rule codes are registered by this task itself beyond what's needed for its own fixtures/tests (e.g., a documented example code) — the actual rule catalog is the scope of the sibling rule-implementation tasks.
- Document the deprecation mechanism (mark deprecated, keep resolvable, never delete) even though no code is deprecated yet.

## Versioned JSON contract

Follow the scene-graph-model precedent exactly: mandatory `schema_version` (start at `1.0`), snake_case keys, explicit discriminators where needed, JSON primitives only, deterministic canonical serialization (stable key ordering, stable list ordering — findings ordered by a documented deterministic sort, e.g. severity then code then first node/beat id — never dict-iteration-order-dependent), byte-identical output for identical report objects, lossless round-trip (`VerificationReport -> JSON -> VerificationReport`), and a checked-in representative fixture (a report with zero findings/`pass=true`, one with only warnings, one with a mix of error and warning findings covering every field including populated and null `t_window`, empty and populated `beat_ids`/`node_ids`) whose exact serialized shape is snapshot-tested to catch accidental key renames, ordering drift, or default-omission regressions. This is the schema every future rule task, the CLI's `verify --json` output, and any external tooling (issue templates per the NFR's "artifact metadata stamp" observability requirement) depends on.

## Human format from the same object

Product-spec capability 7 is explicit: "Human-readable output is a formatting of the same report, never a separate code path." Implement a formatter (e.g. `render_human(report) -> str`) that consumes the exact same `VerificationReport`/`Finding` objects the JSON codec serializes — no parallel human-only data path, no fields computed only for text output. Output must be deterministic (same grouping/sort order as the JSON's findings ordering), clearly separate error-severity findings from warnings, print each finding's code, location (beat/node ids, `t_window` if present), expected vs actual, and `fix_hint`, and print a one-line pass/fail summary derived from `report.pass` and `counts`. Golden/snapshot-test this formatter against the same fixture reports used for the JSON contract test, so a report-model change that breaks one representation is caught by both.

## Render gating with a `--force` trace

Implement a render-gate policy — a small module the not-yet-built render pipeline calls, not a copy of render logic — with this contract: given a `VerificationReport` and a `force: bool`:

- **Zero error-severity findings** (warnings allowed): render proceeds; the render's output metadata stamp records `verification: {status: "passed", warning_count: N}`.
- **≥1 error-severity finding, `force=False`**: render is refused before any rendering work happens. This surfaces through `cli-framework`'s typed exit code 3 (verification failure) with a structured result listing the blocking findings (reusing this task's own report/finding serialization, not a bespoke error shape) — never a raw traceback, per the NFR's "failure = finding" reliability requirement.
- **≥1 error-severity finding, `force=True`**: render proceeds, but the output metadata stamp must carry a visible, non-suppressible trace — `verification: {status: "forced", error_count: N, warning_count: M, bypassed_codes: [...], forced_at: <timestamp>}` — per the NFR's explicit security requirement that "`verify --force`-style escape hatches always leave visible traces in output metadata — the verification promise must not be silently bypassable." There is no way to force a render and have the metadata stamp omit that fact; this must be enforced by a test, not just documented.

Because the real Skia/PyAV render pipeline does not exist yet (it is separate, not-yet-scheduled Phase 1 scope), this task's gating deliverable is tested against fixture `VerificationReport`s and a stand-in render callable/metadata-stamp object — not the real renderer. When the render pipeline task lands, it calls this gate and stamps its real output metadata using the shape this task defines; wiring the gate into the actual rasterizer/encoder is that future task's job, not this one's, and this spec is explicit about that boundary so the gate isn't half-integrated and mistaken for done.

## Explicitly out of scope

- Any real verification rule implementation (`REF.*`, `OVERLAP.*`, `TIMING.*`, `MAP.*`, `CONTINUITY.*`, `PACING.*`, `STYLE.*`, `BUILD.EXCEPTION`, subtitle contrast, etc.) — separate Phase 2 tasks populate `findings[]` against this schema.
- The `anima verify` CLI command's graph-build/timeline-compile/rule-execution pipeline itself — this task supplies the report schema, human formatter, and gate policy that command will use once built.
- The real render pipeline (rasterization, encoding, output metadata stamping beyond the `verification` block's shape) — separate Phase 1 scope.
- Per-project severity promote/demote configuration and its visibility in report metadata — explicitly Phase 5 (`flat` pack + taste constraints) scope per ROADMAP.
- Spatial indexing / performance work for evaluating rules at scale — that's the concern of the rule-implementation and performance tasks, not the report schema itself.

deliverables.description: Immutable, typed VerificationReport and Finding value objects (namespaced/validated `code`, `severity` restricted to error|warning, `beat_ids`, `node_ids`, optional `t_window`, `expected`/`actual`, required non-empty `fix_hint`, `rule_version`; report-level `project`, `engine_version`, `pack_version`, `schema_version`, `counts`, `findings`, and a derived `pass` that is true iff zero error-severity findings and cannot be independently set) plus a small versioned code registry that rejects silent redefinition of an already-registered code.

Public schema-version 1.0 JSON codec for VerificationReport/Finding with deterministic canonical serialization (stable findings ordering and key ordering), lossless round trips, strict malformed/unknown-version handling, and a checked-in representative fixture (empty/pass, warnings-only, and mixed error+warning reports covering every field including null and populated t_window) whose exact serialized form is snapshot-tested.

Human-readable report formatter that renders directly from the same VerificationReport/Finding objects the JSON codec serializes (no parallel data path), grouping errors before warnings in the same deterministic order as the JSON output, printing code/location/expected-actual/fix_hint per finding and a pass/fail summary; golden-tested against the same fixture reports used for the JSON contract test.

Render-gate policy module enforcing: proceed silently on zero error-severity findings; refuse via the CLI's typed exit code 3 with structured blocking-findings output and no raw traceback when errors are present and --force is absent; proceed when --force is present but always attach a non-suppressible `verification` trace block (status, error/warning counts, bypassed codes, timestamp) to the render's output metadata stamp. Tested against fixture reports and a stand-in render/metadata-stamp callable, plus full lint/type/test pass on the new module.

verification.command: pytest -q tests/verification/test_render_gate.py && ruff check src/anima/verification tests/verification && mypy --strict src/anima/verification
